summary:
Henry VIII's Break with Rome (1529–1534 AD) established the Church of England as a national church under royal rather than papal authority, separating England from Catholic Christendom and creating the institutional foundation of Anglicanism — though the immediate cause was not theological conviction but Henry's desire to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon to marry Anne Boleyn. Pope Clement VII refused the annulment (largely because Catherine's nephew, Holy Roman Emperor Charles V, controlled Rome after the Sack of 1527 and would not tolerate the humiliation of his aunt). Henry's solution was to remove papal authority from England entirely: through the Act of Supremacy (1534), he declared himself 'the only supreme head on earth of the Church of England.'

The English Reformation proceeded through Henry's reign as a combination of genuine Protestant reform (dissolution of the monasteries, 1536–1541, which transferred enormous wealth to the crown and its supporters), conservative Catholic theology without papal authority, and brutal suppression of both Catholic resisters (Sir Thomas More and Bishop John Fisher were beheaded for refusing to accept the supremacy) and Protestant radicals. The dissolution of the monasteries was the largest transfer of property in England since the [[event:2234|Norman Conquest]], distributing monastic lands to the gentry and nobility and creating a class with strong financial interests in Protestant settlement. Henry's six wives — Catherine of Aragon (divorced), Anne Boleyn (executed for alleged adultery), Jane Seymour (died in childbirth), Anne of Cleves (divorced), Catherine Howard (executed), Katherine Parr (survived) — have become the most memorable mnemonic in English royal history. Under his successors — the Protestant Edward VI, the Catholic Mary I, and the Protestant [[event:2471|Elizabeth I]] — the English church's doctrinal identity oscillated before settling into a distinctively 'middle way' (via media) between Catholic and Reformed theology.
